sourceAuthority: Dr. Ahmed Hesham — The Tutor Book — OET Writing Samples (Writing 1–6)

samples.title: Routine Referral
samples.caseNotesPdf: Ms Sarah Miller - Case Notes ( Routine Referral to Dermatologist..Pt with Acne & Rosacea ).pdf
samples.modelAnswerPdf: Ms Sarah Miller - Answer Sheet ( Routine Referral to a Dermatologist ).pdf
samples.caseNotesText:
OCCUPATIONAL ENGLISH TEST WRITING SUB-TEST: MEDICINE TIME ALLOWED: READING TIME: 5 MINUTES, WRITING TIME: 40 MINUTES Read the case notes and complete the writing task which follows. Notes PATIENT DETAILS: Name: Ms. Sarah Miller Age: 22/04/1998 - 25 years old Patient history: Occupation: Primary school teacher  Social: Non-smoker, drinks 1-2 units of alcohol/week  Hobbies: Swimming, outdoor hiking Family history: Mother: Adult acne  Father: Hypertension Medical history: Childhood eczema (resolved)  Seasonal allergies (hay fever) 15/09/2023: Subjective: C/O persistent facial breakouts, mainly cheeks and forehead. Duration: 6 months. Used various OTC facial scrubs/washes with no success. Reports skin feels 'tight' and occasionally itchy. Objective: Inflammatory papules and closed comedones on bilateral cheeks. Mild erythema. Temp: 36.8 - Pulse: 72 - BP: 115/75. OET with Dr. Ahmed Hesham

Management: Topical Benzoyl peroxide 5% gel. Advice on gentle cleansing. Review in 4 weeks. 13/10/2023: Subjective: Acne persists; reports 'burning' sensation after applying topical gel. No significant improvement in skin clarity. Feeling self-conscious at work. Objective: Increased redness (erythema). Pustules noted on chin and nose. Dry, flaky skin in treated areas. Management: Discontinue Benzoyl peroxide. Start Topical Clindamycin 1% twice daily. Review in 6 weeks. 20/11/2023: Subjective: Minimal improvement in acne. Reports frequent facial flushing, especially after hot drinks or exercise. Objective: Moderate cystic acne. Persistent background redness on cheeks and nose. Fine telangiectasia noted on nasal alae. Diagnosis: Refractory acne vulgaris. Management: Commenced oral Lymecycline 408mg daily. Advised sun protection. 01/01/2024: Subjective: Patient distressed; acne 'worse than ever'. Persistent stinging and flushing. Desperate for specialist intervention. Objective: Severe cystic acne with scarring. Marked facial erythema and papulopustular lesions. Suspected Rosacea overlap. Plan: Refer to dermatologist for management of severe acne and investigation of rosacea. Writing Task: Refer the patient to a dermatologist for assessment and specialist management of severe acne and possible rosacea. Write the letter to Dr. Helena Vance, Dermatologist, Skin Health Clinic, 45 Central Avenue, Newtown. OET with Dr. Ahmed Hesham

In your answer: • Expand the relevant notes into complete sentences • Do not use note form • Use letter format • The body of the letter should be approximately 180-200 words.  OET with Dr. Ahmed Hesham
samples.modelAnswerText:
Dr. Helena Vance Dermatologist Skin Health Clinic 45 Central Avenue Newtown 01/01/2024 Dear Dr. Vance, Re: Ms. Sarah Miller  DOB: 22/04/1998 I am writing to request your assessment and specialist management of Ms. Miller, a 25-year-old primary school teacher, who has presented with features suggestive of severe acne and suspected rosacea. Ms. Miller initially presented on 15/09/2023 with a six-month history of facial breakouts. She reported itchy, tight skin, for which she used OTC facial scrubs and washes without improvement. Initial management with topical Benzoyl peroxide was unsuccessful, causing burning sensation with increased redness and pustules. A subsequent trial of topical Clindamycin in October failed to improve the condition. In November 2023, Ms. Miller was commenced on oral Lymecycline, 408 mg daily for refractory acne. At that time, she reported frequent facial flushing triggered by exercise and hot drinks. On examination, background erythema and telangiectasia were noted on the cheeks and nose; therefore, she was given advice on sun protection. On today’s visit, Ms. Miller’s condition showed deterioration. Her examination showed severe cystic acne and evidence of scarring in addition to marked facial erythema and papulopustular lesions. OET with Dr. Ahmed Hesham

Ms. Miller’s medical records show that she has a history of childhood eczema and seasonal hay fever. She does not smoke; however, she drinks one to two units of alcohol per week. She has a family history of adult acne in her mother.I would be grateful if you could order further investigations for Ms Miller, if you find appropriate. Should there be any queries, kindly do not hesitate to contact me. Yours sincerely, Doctor OET with Dr. Ahmed Hesham

samples.title: Non-Medical Referral
samples.sourceFolder: Writing 2 ( Referral to non medical profession as occupational therapist - manager of human supportive scheme - psychologist - radiographer )
samples.caseNotesPdf: Arthur Miller ( Human Scheme manager ) Case Notes .pdf
samples.caseNotesText:
2026  OET OCCUPATIONAL ENGLISH TEST WRITING SUB-TEST - TEST BOOKLET __________________________________________________ WRITING SUB-TEST: MEDICINE TIME ALLOWED: READING TIME: 5 MINUTES | WRITING TIME: 40 MINUTES  Read the case notes and complete the writing task which follows.  Assume that Today’s date is 12th January 2024 Notes  PATIENT DETAILS: Name: Mr. Arthur Miller Age: 41 years old Patient history: • Residence: Disability center (since May 2022) • Social history: Smoker; enjoys computer games; socially restricted • Support: Supported by his sister • Medical: Moderate learning disability Family history: • Mother: Parkinsonism (Deceased Nov 2023) • Father: Schizophrenia 

  15/03/2023: Subjective: Low mood, loss of appetite, insomnia. Management: Prescribed SSRI and Temazepam to help sleep 20/08/2023: Subjective: No mood improvement; constipation reported (S.E of medication). Objective: Pallor noted; Iron studies ordered Assessment: Low iron result Management: Prescribed iron tablets; advised on hydration; continue SSRI.  12/01/2024: Subjective: Insomnia improved; persistent low mood (bereavement following mother loss). Objective: Poor diet (lacks Vegetables / Meat); socially withdrawn Psychotherapy appointment scheduled for 15/02 at the care home, once weekly for six weeks.  Plan: Advised on dietary improvements (cereals/veg/meat). Refer to Human Supportive Scheme for activity support; medication monitoring; review 26/01. Consent given for current plan.  __________________________________________________ Writing Task: Using the information given in the case notes, write a letter of referral to the Manager of the Human Supportive Scheme at the care home. Request further evaluation, support with daily activities, and monitoring of medication compliance.  Write this letter to Mr Alex Robertson, Manager at Human Supportive Scheme, Newtown Care Home, 222 West Road, Newtown.  In your answer: • Expand relevant notes into complete sentences • Do not use note form 

  • Use letter format • Body length: 180-200 words approx.
samples.modelAnswerText:
Mr. Alex Robertson Manager at Human Supportive Scheme Newtown Care Home 222 West Road Newtown   12 January 2024  Dear Mr. Robertson, RE: Mr. Arthur Miller, aged 41  I am writing to refer Mr. Miller, who has a diagnosis of moderate learning disability, and has been residing at your disability centre since May 2022, for further evaluation and support with his daily activities.   Following the loss of his mother in November 2023, Mr. Miller has experienced persistent low mood and has become increasingly socially withdrawn. While his insomnia has shown improvement with a previous medication for sleeping difficulty, his mood remains low despite the use of an antidepressant. Please note, a six-week course of psychotherapy has been arranged to start at the care home on 15 February.  Socially, Mr. Miller is supported by his sister and has a keen interest in computer games. In addition, his diet is currently inadequate, lacking sufficient vegetables and meat, which has resulted in low iron levels. Subsequently, he was commenced on iron supplements.   I would be grateful if you could take over Mr Miller’s dietary improvements and monitor his compliance with all prescribed medications. I intend to review his progress on 26 January, and he has provided informed consent for this plan.  

  Should there be any queries, kindly do not hesitate to contact me.  Yours sincerely, Doctor

samples.title: Urgent Referral
samples.modelAnswerPdf: Leo Bennett ( Urgent Referral ) - Answer Sheet.pdf
samples.caseNotesText:
49 +44 7961 725989 OCCUPATIONAL ENGLISH TEST WRITING SUB-TEST: MEDICINE  TIME ALLOWED:     PATIENT DETAILS:     Patient history:    Social:  Family history:   Medical history:   22/12/2023:  Subjective:   Duration:   Objective:  Full neurological exam:   Management:    31/12/2023:  Subjective:   Objective (ED):   Management:      READING TIME: 5 MINUTES WRITING TIME: 40 MINUTES    Name: Leo Bennett AGE: 12/05/2016 - 7 years old Mother: Present (Mrs. Bennett)   Previously well child living with parents. Normal developmental milestones. No regular medications or prior seizures.   Year 2 student. Active; attends weekly swimming lessons.  Maternal uncle: Epilepsy (well-controlled).  Nil significant. No history of head trauma.    C/O sudden 'funny feeling' (tingling) in R hand while swimming.   30s. No LOC.  Child alert and oriented.   Normal.   Suspected transient sensory episode. Advised diary.    Facial drooping and speech arrest (1 min) at home. Attended ED.  Post-ictal fatigue. Clinical exam unremarkable. BP: 110/70. Vitals stable.  Reassured by staff; suspected 'atypical migraine'. Follow-up GP.      

 50 +44 7961 725989  08/01/2024  Subjective:   Witnessed Event:  Objective:  Duration:    Post-ictal:   Neurological exam (post-ictal):   Diagnosis:    Plan:         Attended GP clinic with mother. Third episode occurred during consultation.  Rhythmic twitching of R mouth. Speech arrest.  Conscious. Profuse drooling.  90s.  Mild drowsiness.   Normal.    ? Focal Onset Seizures (Rolandic Epilepsy).   Urgent referral to Pediatric Neurologist for EEG/Management.     Writing Task: Write a referral letter to Dr. Sarah Jennings, a Pediatric Neurologist, for specialist assessment of focal seizures. Address the letter to Dr. Sarah Jennings, Pediatric Neurologist, Children’s Specialty Centre, 88 Healthcare Lane, Newtown.  In your answer: • Expand the relevant notes into complete sentences • Do not use note form • Use letter format  The body of the letter should be approximately 180-200 words.
samples.modelAnswerText:
Dr Sarah Jennings Pediatric Neurologist Children's Specialty Centre 88 Healthcare Lane Newtown  08/01/2024  Dear Dr Jennings, RE: Leo Bennett                                                                          DOB: 12/05/2016                                                                                                          I am writing to request urgent management and EEG for Leo, a 7-year-old boy who has presented with features suggestive of focal onset seizures, possibly Rolandic epilepsy.   Today, Leo experienced a third episode which was witnessed during the consultation. This event lasted ninety seconds and was characterized by rhythmic twitching of the right side of his mouth, speech arrest and profuse drooling. Although he remained conscious, he was mildly drowsy post-ictally. His neurological examination following the episode was normal.   Leo’s symptoms initially started on 22/12/2023 with a thirty-second sensory episode involving tingling in his right hand while swimming without losing consciousness. His neurological examination was normal; therefore, he was advised to keep a diary.  

  On the following visit in the same month, Leo experienced facial drooping and speech arrest for one minute. He was reviewed in the Emergency Department at that time, where his assessment was unremarkable, and atypical migraine was suspected.  Leo is a well child, living with his parents with no significant medical history or prior head trauma. He has normal developmental milestones. He is not currently on any regular medications. Please note, his maternal uncle has well-controlled epilepsy.  I would be grateful if you could assess Leo at your earliest convenience. Should there be any queries, kindly do not hesitate to contact me.  Yours sincerely,  Doctor

samples.title: Update & Discharge to GP
samples.caseNotesPdf: Mr Liam Sterling - Case Notes ( Update & Discharge Letter ).pdf
samples.modelAnswerPdf: Mr Sterling - Answer Sheet ( Update & Discharge letter ).pdf
samples.caseNotesText:
OET with Dr. Ahmed Hesham WRITING SUB-TEST - MEDICINE  Notes  Assume that today's date is 08/01/2024  You are the Surgical Registrar at St. Jude’s Public Hospital  Patient Details Name: Mr Liam Sterling Date of Birth: 15/11/1982 (41 years old)  Patient History • Freelance Graphic Designer • Lives with partner and young daughter • Amateur photographer and long-distance cyclist • Social drinker (1-2 beers/weekend) • Non-smoker • Tonsillectomy (2001) • Seasonal hay fever (managed with OTC antihistamines) • Allergy: PENICILLIN (Reaction: Urticarial rash)  Family History • Father: Osteoarthritis • Mother: Hyperthyroidism • Brother: Eczema  Medications • OTC antihistamines (as required)  05/01/2024 C/O • Sudden onset of severe, colicky RUQ pain (10 hours) • Radiation to the right shoulder blade • Nausea and bilious vomiting (x1) post-spicy meal  Examination • Tenderness in the RUQ 

 OET with Dr. Ahmed Hesham • Positive Murphy’s Sign • Temperature: 38.3°C; Pulse: 95 bpm; Blood Pressure: 130/80 mmHg  Investigations • Ultrasound: Thickened gallbladder wall (5mm), pericholecystic fluid, and multiple mobile gallstones.  06/01/2024 Laparoscopic cholecystectomy (uncomplicated) C/O • Post-operative pain (5/10) • Mild sore throat • Anxiety regarding a scheduled photography workshop  Examination • Surgery uncomplicated • Stable vitals; lungs clear  Plan • Commence oral fluids • Encourage mobilization with assistance  08/01/2024 C/O • "Itchy and tight" feeling at the umbilical site  Examination • Umbilical port: Erythema, localized swelling, and clear discharge • Other port sites: Healing well • Abdomen: Soft but slightly bloated • Temperature: 37.5°C; Pulse: 82 bpm; Blood Pressure: 128/82 mmHg  Diagnosis • Superficial Surgical Site Infection (SSI) 

 OET with Dr. Ahmed Hesham  Management • Diagnosis explained to patient • Prescribed Clindamycin 300mg QID for 7 days (Alternative due to Penicillin allergy) • Wound dressed  Plan • Discharge today • Refer to GP (Dr Sarah Jenkins) for definitive wound management and removal of non-dissolvable sutures in 5 days • Advised to avoid heavy lifting and long-distance cycling for 6 weeks   Writing Task "Using the information given in the case notes, write a letter of discharge to Dr Sarah Jenkins. Address the letter to Dr Sarah Jenkins, City Medical Centre, 45 Orchard Lane, Newtown."   In your answer: • Expand relevant notes into complete sentences • Do not use note form • Use letter format • Body length: 180-200 words approx.
samples.modelAnswerText:
Dr Sarah Jenkins City Medical Centre 45 Orchard Lane Newtown 08/01/2024 Dear Dr Jenkins, RE: Mr Liam Sterling  DOB: 15/11/1982 I am writing to update you on Mr Sterling who is being discharged today from our hospital into your care following uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis. Your follow-up and ongoing care would be highly appreciated. Mr Sterling was admitted on 05/01/2024 with a ten-hour history of severe, colicky, right upper quadrant pain radiating to the right shoulder blade, which was associated with nausea and vomiting. Clinical examination revealed right upper quadrant tenderness, a temperature of 38.3°C and positive Murphy’s sign. An ultrasound scan confirmed the diagnosis of acute cholecystitis, showing multiple gallstones, a thickened gallbladder wall, and pericholecystic fluid. Mr Sterling underwent an uncomplicated surgical procedure the following day. His postoperative recovery was initially unremarkable, and he was commenced on oral fluids and advised to mobilize with assistance. Today, Mr Sterling reported an itchy and tight sensation in the umbilical area. This was evidenced by mild erythema, localized swelling, clear discharge and a temperature of 37.5°C; however, other port sites were healing well. His examination showed soft, slightly bloated abdomen. Subsequently, he was diagnosed with surgical site infection. Management has been initiated with a seven-day course of oral Clindamycin, 300 mg four times a day due to his penicillin allergy in addition to wound dressing. 

  It would be appreciated if you could review Mr Sterling’s wound in five days and remove his non-dissolvable sutures. Please note, he has been advised to avoid heavy lifting and long-distance cycling for six weeks.  Should there be any queries, please do not hesitate to contact me.  Yours sincerely,  Surgical Registrar

samples.title: Update & Referral (Specialist to GP/Dentist)
samples.sourceFolder: Writing 5 ( Update & Referral - From Specialist to GP or Dentist )
samples.caseNotesPdf: Mr Adam Wilson - Case Notes ( Update & Referral from ENT to Dentist ).pdf
samples.modelAnswerPdf: Mr Adam Wilson - Answer Sheet ( Update & Referral from ENT to Dentist ).pdf
samples.caseNotesText:
WRITING SUB-TEST – MEDICINE  Notes Assume that today’s date is 08/01/2024 You are Dr Sarah Mitchell, an ENT Specialist --- Patient Details • Name: Mr Adam Wilson • Date of Birth: 15/03/1982 (42 years old) --- Patient History • Social drinker • Non-smoker --- Family History • Father: Myocardial infarction • Mother: Type 2 diabetes mellitus --- Medical History • Type 2 diabetes mellitus × 5 years • Hypertension  --- Medications: - Metformin / Norvasc 

  --- 07/2023 Left upper molar root canal treatment  08/01/2024  •  C/O left facial pain × 3 months • Persistent unilateral nasal discharge (L) • Foul nasal smell • Post-nasal drip • Pain worse on bending forward • No fever • No epistaxis • No visual disturbance • Recurrent dental infection • Multiple courses oral antibiotics → minimal improvement --- Examination • Facial tenderness over the left maxillary sinus • Purulent discharge observed in the left middle meatus during nasal endoscopy • Tenderness over the left upper molar region • Temperature: 36.8°C; Pulse: 78 bpm; Blood Pressure: 125/85 mmHg --- Investigations • CT PNS: Opacification of the left maxillary sinus. Evidence of periapical dental infection communicating with the sinus. 

  --- Diagnosis • Odontogenic maxillary sinusitis --- Management • Diagnosis explained to patient • Advised nasal saline irrigation • Analgesia recommended • Treatment of dental source indicated --- Plan • Refer to family dentist for definitive dental management ± dental surgery • Consider combined ENT–dental intervention if symptoms persist   Writing Task  “Using the information given in the case notes, write a letter of referral to Dr James Carter. Address the letter to Dr James Carter, Family Dentist, Riverside Dental Practice, 45 High Street, London.”
samples.modelAnswerText:
Dr James Carter Family Dentist Riverside Dental Practice 45 High Street London  08/01/2024  Dear Dr Carter, Re: Mr Adam Wilson                                                           DOB: 15/03/1982  I am writing to update you on the recent ENT consultation of Mr Wilson, who will be referred back into your care following his diagnosis with odontogenic maxillary sinusitis. Your further dental management would be highly appreciated.  Today, Mr Wilson presented with a three-month history of left-sided facial pain increasing on bending forward, persistent unilateral nasal discharge and a foul nasal odour, which have not responded to repeated courses of oral antibiotics. These symptoms developed following a previous root canal treatment of his left upper molar. Please note, he denied having fever, epistaxis or visual disturbance.  On examination, there was tenderness over the left maxillary sinus, and purulent discharge was noted in the left middle meatus on nasal endoscopy. A CT scan of the paranasal sinuses demonstrated opacification of the left maxillary sinus with evidence of a periapical dental infection communicating with the sinus. Subsequently, Mr Wilson has been advised 

  to commence regular nasal saline irrigation and simple analgesia. However, definitive management requires treatment of the underlying dental source.   Mr Wilson’s medical records show that he has type two diabetes mellitus and hypertension, for which he is on metformin and Norvasc. He drinks socially; however, he does not smoke.  I would be grateful if you could consider surgical intervention for Mr Wilson, if you find appropriate. If his symptoms persist despite dental treatment, combined ENT and dental surgical management may be required.  Should there be any queries, kindly do not hesitate to contact me.  Yours sincerely,  Dr Sarah Mitchell
